sources.notes: Used to confirm the Sprague-Grundy decomposition, normalization to (q,k), period 2^(k+1), and prefix-XOR computation.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: Determine the winner of a disjoint sum of impartial games defined by arithmetic-progressions and an interval of possible upper bounds.
annotation.constraints: The intervals and values can be large, so each game must be reduced to logarithmic-time Grundy range XOR instead of enumerating all upper bounds.
annotation.core_idea: By Sprague-Grundy, XOR the contribution of each independent series. Normalize one game by shifting a to 0; because the three numbers form an arithmetic progression, the state can be represented by remaining slack t and step d. Writing d=2^k D with D odd and q=floor(t/D), the Grundy number depends only on (q,k). Its recurrence reduces to two mex terms, and for fixed k the sequence in q is periodic with period 2^(k+1). Therefore the XOR over a large interval is reduced to boundary odd-length blocks plus a prefix-XOR function f(u,k), computed recursively in O(k).
annotation.complexity: O(n log V) per test case.

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Map each interval to q-blocks under d=2^kD, handle boundary blocks by parity, and compute full q-ranges with recursive periodic prefix XOR of Grundy values.

tags.evidence: The winner is decided by XORing the Grundy values of independent series.
tags.description: Use Sprague-Grundy numbers and xor-sum for impartial combinatorial games.
tags.created_reason: Generic game theory is too broad for impartial-game xor-sum problems.

tags.evidence: For fixed k, g(q,k) is periodic with period 2^(k+1), enabling logarithmic prefix XOR.
tags.description: Exploit periodic or eventually periodic behavior of states, sequences, or DP values.
tags.created_reason: Periodicity is a common decisive reduction and should be searchable separately.

tags.evidence: Range XOR of Grundy values is answered as prefix_grundy(r,k) xor prefix_grundy(l-1,k).
The original parameters collapse to the quotient state (q,k).
The exponent k is obtained from the power of two dividing d.
